name: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an SQL Command ('SQL Injection')
description: The product constructs all or part of an SQL command using externally-influenced input from an upstream component, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes special elements that could modify the intended SQL command when it is sent to a downstream component. Without sufficient removal or quoting of SQL syntax in user-controllable inputs, the generated SQL query can cause those inputs to be interpreted as SQL instead of ordinary user data.
common_consequences: Remote code execution via xp_cmdshell or similar
mitigations: Use parameterized queries or prepared statements
Use stored procedures
Apply input validation with allowlists
Escape all user-supplied input
Apply least privilege to database accounts
Use an ORM with parameterized query support

name: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an OS Command ('OS Command Injection')
description: The product constructs all or part of an OS command using externally-influenced input from an upstream component, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes special elements that could modify the intended OS command when it is sent to a downstream component. This allows attackers to execute arbitrary commands on the host operating system.
mitigations: Avoid calling OS commands directly
Use language-specific APIs instead of shell commands
If OS commands are required, use strict allowlists for input validation
Do not pass user input directly to shell interpreters
Use parameterized command interfaces

name: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in a Command ('Command Injection')
description: The product constructs all or part of a command using externally-influenced input from an upstream component, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes special elements that could modify the intended command when it is sent to a downstream component. This is the general class of command injection, encompassing OS command injection and other command interpreters.
mitigations: Avoid invoking commands through shell interpreters
Use parameterized APIs
Validate and sanitize all input
Use allowlists for acceptable input values

name: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an LDAP Query ('LDAP Injection')
description: The product constructs all or part of an LDAP query using externally-influenced input from an upstream component, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes special elements that could modify the intended LDAP query when it is sent to a downstream component.
mitigations: Validate and escape all user input before constructing LDAP queries
Use frameworks that automatically protect against LDAP injection
Apply least privilege to LDAP service accounts
Use parameterized LDAP queries where possible

name: Improper Neutralization of Data within XPath Expressions ('XPath Injection')
description: The product uses external input to dynamically construct an XPath expression used to retrieve data from an XML document, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes that input, allowing an attacker to control the structure of the query.
mitigations: Use parameterized XPath queries
Validate and sanitize user input
Use precompiled XPath expressions
Apply input allowlists

name: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements in Data Query Logic
description: The product generates a query intended to access or manipulate data in a data store such as a database, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes special elements that can modify the intended logic of the query. This includes NoSQL injection attacks against MongoDB, CouchDB, and similar databases.
mitigations: Use parameterized queries or ODM/ORM libraries
Validate input types strictly
Sanitize user input before constructing queries
Use allowlists for query operators

name: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)
description: The web server receives a URL or similar request from an upstream component and retrieves the contents of this URL, but it does not sufficiently ensure that the request is being sent to the expected destination. By providing URLs to unexpected hosts or ports, attackers can make it appear that the server is sending the request, possibly bypassing access controls such as firewalls.
common_consequences: Remote code execution via internal services
mitigations: Validate and sanitize all URLs
Use allowlists for permitted domains and IP ranges
Block requests to private/internal IP ranges
Disable unnecessary URL schemes (file://, gopher://, etc.)
Use network-level segmentation
Do not expose raw responses to users

name: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements Used in a Template Engine
description: The product uses a template engine to insert or process externally-influenced input, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes special elements or syntax that can be interpreted as template directives. This leads to Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI) which can result in remote code execution.
mitigations: Use logic-less template engines when possible
Sandbox template execution environments
Never pass user input directly into template strings
Validate and sanitize all template inputs
Use allowlists for template variables

name: Improper Control of Generation of Code ('Code Injection')
description: The product constructs all or part of a code segment using externally-influenced input from an upstream component, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes special elements that could modify the syntax or behavior of the intended code segment. This allows attackers to inject and execute arbitrary code.
mitigations: Avoid using dynamic code evaluation functions (eval, exec, etc.)
Use sandboxed execution environments
Apply strict input validation
Use allowlists for permitted code constructs

name: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an Expression Language Statement ('Expression Language Injection')
description: The product constructs all or part of an expression language (EL) statement in a framework such as a Java Server Page (JSP) using externally-influenced input, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes special elements that could modify the intended EL statement.
mitigations: Avoid using user input in EL expressions
Use parameterized interfaces
Validate and sanitize input
Upgrade to frameworks that disable EL evaluation in dangerous contexts

name: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')
description: The product does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes user-controllable input before it is placed in output that is used as a web page that is served to other users. This is the general entry for XSS covering reflected, stored, and DOM-based variants.
mitigations: Encode output based on context (HTML, JavaScript, URL, CSS)
Use Content Security Policy (CSP) headers
Use HTTPOnly and Secure cookie flags
Validate and sanitize input
Use auto-escaping template engines

name: Improper Neutralization of Script-Related HTML Tags in a Web Page (Basic XSS)
description: The product receives input from an upstream component, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes special characters such as '<', '>', and '&' that could be interpreted as web-scripting elements when they are sent to a downstream web component that processes web pages.
mitigations: HTML-encode all user-supplied output
Use allowlists for permitted HTML tags
Implement Content Security Policy
Use auto-escaping template engines

name: Reflected Cross-site Scripting
description: The application includes unvalidated and unescaped user input as part of HTTP responses. Reflected XSS occurs when the injected script is reflected off the web server, such as in error messages, search results, or any other response that includes input sent to the server as part of the request. The attack is delivered to victims via another route, such as a malicious link in an email or on another website.
mitigations: Encode all output based on context
Validate input against strict allowlists
Use Content Security Policy headers
Set HTTPOnly flag on session cookies
Use modern frameworks with auto-escaping

name: Stored Cross-site Scripting
description: The application stores user-supplied input that is not properly sanitized, and later includes that input in HTTP responses served to other users. The malicious script is permanently stored on the target servers (in a database, forum post, comment field, etc.) and is served to victims when they view the affected page. This variant is more dangerous than reflected XSS because it does not require social engineering to deliver the payload.
mitigations: Sanitize all user input before storage
Encode output based on context
Use Content Security Policy
Implement input length limits
Use auto-escaping template engines

name: DOM-Based Cross-site Scripting
description: The application's client-side scripts write user-controllable data to the Document Object Model (DOM) without proper sanitization. The attack payload is executed as a result of modifying the DOM in the victim's browser, so the malicious content never reaches the server. Sources include document.location, document.URL, document.referrer, and window.name.
mitigations: Avoid using dangerous DOM sinks (innerHTML, document.write, eval)
Use safe DOM APIs (textContent, setAttribute)
Sanitize data from DOM sources before use
Use Trusted Types API
Implement Content Security Policy

name: Improper Authentication
description: When an actor claims to have a given identity, the product does not prove or insufficiently proves that the claim is correct. This can allow attackers to gain access to resources or functionality that should require authenticated access.
mitigations: Use well-vetted authentication libraries and frameworks
Implement multi-factor authentication
Use strong password policies
Implement account lockout mechanisms
Log and monitor authentication failures
Use secure session management

name: Missing Authentication for Critical Function
description: The product does not perform any authentication for functionality that requires a provable user identity or consumes a significant amount of resources. Attackers can access critical functions without any credentials.
mitigations: Require authentication for all critical functions
Implement centralized authentication enforcement
Use middleware or filters to enforce authentication globally
Conduct threat modeling to identify all critical functions

name: Use of Hard-coded Credentials
description: The product contains hard-coded credentials, such as a password or cryptographic key, which it uses for its own inbound authentication, outbound communication to external components, or encryption of internal data. Hard-coded credentials create a significant security hole that allows an attacker to bypass authentication.
mitigations: Store credentials in secure configuration files outside the source code
Use environment variables or secrets management systems
Use key vaults or HSMs for cryptographic keys
Implement credential rotation
Use per-installation unique credentials

name: Insufficiently Protected Credentials
description: The product transmits or stores authentication credentials, but it uses an insecure method that is susceptible to unauthorized interception and/or retrieval. This includes storing passwords in plaintext, using weak hashing algorithms, or transmitting credentials over unencrypted channels.
mitigations: Use strong adaptive hashing algorithms (bcrypt, scrypt, Argon2) for password storage
Transmit credentials only over encrypted channels (TLS)
Never store passwords in plaintext or with reversible encryption
Use salted hashes
Implement proper key derivation functions

name: Improper Restriction of Excessive Authentication Attempts
description: The product does not implement sufficient measures to prevent multiple failed authentication attempts within a short time frame, making it more susceptible to brute force attacks on passwords, PINs, or other credentials.
mitigations: Implement account lockout after a threshold of failed attempts
Use progressive delays between attempts
Implement CAPTCHA after failed attempts
Use rate limiting
Monitor and alert on brute force patterns

name: Session Fixation
description: Authenticating a user, or otherwise establishing a new user session, without invalidating any existing session identifier gives an attacker the opportunity to steal authenticated sessions. The attacker can force a known session ID on the victim before authentication, and then hijack the session after the victim authenticates.
mitigations: Regenerate session IDs upon authentication
Invalidate existing sessions on login
Use framework-provided session management
Set session cookies with Secure and HttpOnly flags
Implement session timeout

name: Insufficient Session Expiration
description: The product does not sufficiently expire or invalidate session identifiers, which can allow attackers to use old session credentials or session IDs for authorization. Failing to expire sessions properly allows session hijacking attacks to succeed over extended periods.
mitigations: Implement absolute session timeouts
Implement idle session timeouts
Invalidate sessions on logout
Invalidate sessions on password change
Use short-lived tokens with refresh mechanisms

name: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF)
description: The web application does not, or can not, sufficiently verify whether a well-formed, valid, consistent request was intentionally provided by the user who submitted the request. This allows a malicious site to transmit unauthorized commands on behalf of an authenticated user.
mitigations: Use anti-CSRF tokens (synchronizer tokens)
Verify Origin and Referer headers
Use SameSite cookie attribute
Require re-authentication for sensitive operations
Use custom request headers for AJAX

name: Sensitive Cookie in HTTPS Session Without 'Secure' Attribute
description: The Secure attribute for sensitive cookies in HTTPS sessions is not set, which could cause the user agent to send those cookies in plaintext over an HTTP session. This can expose session tokens and other sensitive data.
mitigations: Set the Secure flag on all sensitive cookies
Set the HttpOnly flag on session cookies
Use SameSite attribute
Enforce HTTPS-only communication with HSTS

name: Missing Authorization
description: The product does not perform an authorization check when an actor attempts to access a resource or perform an action. This allows attackers to access data or perform actions that they should not be allowed to.
mitigations: Implement authorization checks for every access to protected resources
Use a centralized authorization mechanism
Apply deny-by-default access control
Use role-based access control (RBAC) or attribute-based access control (ABAC)
Conduct thorough access control testing

name: Incorrect Authorization
description: The product performs an authorization check when an actor attempts to access a resource or perform an action, but it does not correctly perform the check. This allows attackers to bypass intended access restrictions.
mitigations: Use a centralized and well-tested authorization library
Implement authorization checks on the server side
Use positive authorization checks (verify permission before granting access)
Test authorization logic thoroughly
Implement proper role hierarchy

name: Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key (IDOR)
description: The system's authorization functionality does not prevent one user from gaining access to another user's data or record by modifying the key value identifying the data. This is commonly known as Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR). Attackers can simply modify parameter values (IDs, filenames, etc.) to access resources belonging to other users.
mitigations: Use indirect references that map to server-side resources
Verify authorization for every resource access
Use per-user or per-session indirect reference maps
Implement object-level authorization checks
Use UUIDs instead of sequential IDs

name: Improper Privilege Management
description: The product does not properly assign, modify, track, or check privileges for an actor, creating an unintended sphere of control for that actor. This can lead to privilege escalation where a user gains elevated access they should not have.
mitigations: Apply the principle of least privilege
Implement proper role separation
Regularly audit privilege assignments
Use mandatory access controls
Implement privilege bracketing (drop privileges when not needed)

name: Improper Access Control
description: The product does not restrict or incorrectly restricts access to a resource from an unauthorized actor. Access control involves the use of several protection mechanisms such as authentication, authorization, and accountability. When any of these mechanisms fail, an attacker can compromise the security of the product.
mitigations: Implement defense in depth for access control
Use deny-by-default policies
Apply the principle of least privilege
Centralize access control logic
Test access control thoroughly

name: Use of a Broken or Risky Cryptographic Algorithm
description: The product uses a broken or risky cryptographic algorithm or protocol. Algorithms such as DES, MD5, SHA-1, RC4, and others have known weaknesses that can be exploited by attackers to compromise the confidentiality or integrity of protected data.
mitigations: Use current recommended algorithms (AES-256, SHA-256/SHA-3, RSA-2048+, Ed25519)
Follow NIST or industry guidelines for algorithm selection
Regularly review and update cryptographic implementations
Use well-vetted cryptographic libraries
Plan for crypto agility

name: Inadequate Encryption Strength
description: The product stores or transmits sensitive data using an encryption scheme that is theoretically sound but is not strong enough for the level of protection required. For example, using a key size that is too small (e.g., 56-bit DES, 1024-bit RSA) or an insufficient number of rounds.
mitigations: Use key sizes recommended by current standards (AES-256, RSA-2048+, ECC-256+)
Follow NIST SP 800-131A recommendations
Regularly review key sizes against evolving computational capabilities
Plan for post-quantum cryptography migration

name: Use of Hard-coded Password
description: The product contains a hard-coded password that is used for authentication of the product itself or for communication with an external component. Hard-coded passwords are a particular form of hard-coded credentials.
mitigations: Store passwords in secure external configuration
Use environment variables or secrets management services
Implement secure credential provisioning
Use certificate-based authentication where possible

name: Use of Hard-coded Cryptographic Key
description: The product uses a hard-coded cryptographic key to encrypt or decrypt data, or to generate/verify digital signatures. If the key is discovered, an attacker can decrypt all data encrypted with that key or forge signatures.
mitigations: Generate keys dynamically or store them securely outside the application
Use key management systems (KMS)
Implement key rotation mechanisms
Use hardware security modules (HSMs) for key storage

name: Missing Encryption of Sensitive Data
description: The product does not encrypt sensitive or critical information before storage or transmission. This exposes the data to unauthorized access if the storage or communication channel is compromised.
mitigations: Encrypt all sensitive data at rest and in transit
Use TLS for all network communications
Encrypt databases or sensitive columns
Use full-disk encryption for storage
Classify data to identify what needs encryption

name: Cleartext Storage of Sensitive Information
description: The product stores sensitive information in cleartext within a resource that might be accessible to another control sphere. This includes storing passwords, tokens, credit card numbers, or other sensitive data in plaintext in databases, files, or logs.
mitigations: Encrypt sensitive data before storage
Use strong hashing for passwords (bcrypt, Argon2)
Implement data classification policies
Use column-level encryption for sensitive database fields
Avoid logging sensitive information

name: Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Information
description: The product transmits sensitive or security-critical data in cleartext in a communication channel that can be sniffed by unauthorized actors. This includes transmitting passwords, session tokens, or personal data over unencrypted HTTP connections.
mitigations: Use TLS/HTTPS for all sensitive data transmission
Implement HSTS (HTTP Strict Transport Security)
Use certificate pinning for mobile applications
Encrypt data at the application layer for additional protection

name: Use of Insufficiently Random Values
description: The product uses insufficiently random numbers or values in a security context that depends on unpredictable numbers. This includes using weak pseudo-random number generators (PRNGs) for generating session tokens, cryptographic keys, passwords, or nonces.
mitigations: Use cryptographically secure random number generators (CSPRNG)
Use /dev/urandom or equivalent OS-provided secure random sources
Use language-provided secure random APIs (SecureRandom, crypto.randomBytes, secrets module)
Never use Math.random() or similar for security purposes

name: Improper Verification of Cryptographic Signature
description: The product does not verify, or incorrectly verifies, the cryptographic signature for data. This can allow an attacker to modify data or forge signatures, undermining the integrity guarantees that the signature was intended to provide.
mitigations: Always verify signatures before trusting signed data
Use well-tested cryptographic libraries for signature verification
Validate the entire certificate chain
Check for signature algorithm confusion attacks

name: Improper Certificate Validation
description: The product does not validate, or incorrectly validates, a certificate. This can allow an attacker to spoof a trusted entity by interfering in the communication path between the host and client, typically via man-in-the-middle attacks.
mitigations: Properly validate all certificate fields
Verify the certificate chain to a trusted root CA
Check certificate revocation status (OCSP/CRL)
Implement certificate pinning for critical connections
Never disable certificate validation in production

name: Improper Limitation of a Pathname to a Restricted Directory ('Path Traversal')
description: The product uses external input to construct a pathname that is intended to identify a file or directory that is located underneath a restricted parent directory, but the product does not properly neutralize special elements within the pathname that can cause the pathname to resolve to a location that is outside of the restricted directory. Common attack strings include '../' sequences.
mitigations: Canonicalize paths and validate against the intended directory
Use a chroot jail or similar sandboxing
Reject input containing path traversal characters
Use allowlists for permitted filenames
Use language APIs that prevent path traversal

name: Buffer Copy without Checking Size of Input ('Classic Buffer Overflow')
description: The product copies an input buffer to an output buffer without verifying that the size of the input buffer is less than the size of the output buffer, leading to a buffer overflow. This is one of the most common and dangerous vulnerabilities in C/C++ programs.
mitigations: Use safe string handling functions (strncpy, snprintf instead of strcpy, sprintf)
Use memory-safe languages when possible
Implement bounds checking
Use compiler protections (stack canaries, ASLR, DEP/NX)
Use static analysis tools to detect buffer overflows

name: Out-of-bounds Write
description: The product writes data past the end, or before the beginning, of the intended buffer. This can result in corruption of data, a crash, or execution of arbitrary code. This is the most prevalent dangerous weakness, consistently ranked #1 on the CWE Top 25.
mitigations: Use memory-safe languages
Implement bounds checking on all buffer operations
Use safe library functions
Enable compiler security features (ASLR, stack canaries, DEP)
Use AddressSanitizer during testing

name: Out-of-bounds Read
description: The product reads data past the end, or before the beginning, of the intended buffer. This can allow attackers to read sensitive information from other memory locations or cause a crash.
mitigations: Validate all array and buffer indices
Use memory-safe languages
Implement proper bounds checking
Use AddressSanitizer during testing

name: Integer Overflow or Wraparound
description: The product performs a calculation that can produce an integer overflow or wraparound, when the logic assumes that the resulting value will always be larger than the original value. This can introduce other weaknesses when the calculation is used for resource management or execution control.
mitigations: Use safe integer arithmetic libraries
Check for overflow before performing operations
Use languages with built-in overflow detection
Use larger data types when possible
Validate input ranges

name: Unrestricted Upload of File with Dangerous Type
description: The product allows the upload of files without sufficient validation of the file type, which can lead to execution of arbitrary code when the uploaded file is accessed or processed. Attackers can upload web shells, executables, or other dangerous files.
mitigations: Validate file types using both extension and content (magic bytes)
Store uploaded files outside the web root
Use a separate domain for serving uploaded content
Rename files to prevent execution
Scan uploaded files for malware
Implement file size limits
Use Content-Disposition: attachment for downloads

name: Improper Input Validation
description: The product receives input or data, but it does not validate or incorrectly validates that the input has the properties that are required to process the data safely and correctly. This is the general category for all input validation issues.
mitigations: Validate all input against strict specifications
Use allowlists over denylists
Validate on the server side
Validate data type, length, range, and format
Use strong typing
Reject unexpected input rather than attempting to sanitize

name: Improper Encoding or Escaping of Output
description: The product prepares a structured message for communication with another component, but encoding or escaping of the data is either missing or done incorrectly. As a result, the intended structure of the message is not preserved, leading to injection vulnerabilities.
mitigations: Apply output encoding appropriate to the context (HTML, JavaScript, URL, SQL, OS command)
Use framework-provided encoding functions
Apply encoding as late as possible, just before output
Use parameterized interfaces when available

name: URL Redirection to Untrusted Site ('Open Redirect')
description: A web application accepts a user-controlled input that specifies a link to an external site, and uses that link in a Redirect. This simplifies phishing attacks by allowing attackers to create URLs on the trusted domain that redirect to malicious sites.
mitigations: Avoid using user-controlled input in redirects
Use a mapping of allowed redirect destinations
Validate redirect URLs against an allowlist of trusted domains
Use relative URLs for internal redirects
Warn users before redirecting to external sites

name: Sensitive Cookie Without 'HttpOnly' Flag
description: The product uses a cookie to store sensitive information, but the cookie is not marked with the HttpOnly flag. This allows client-side scripts (JavaScript) to access the cookie, which can be exploited through XSS attacks to steal session tokens.
mitigations: Set the HttpOnly flag on all sensitive cookies
Set the Secure flag for HTTPS-only transmission
Use SameSite attribute to prevent CSRF
Minimize data stored in cookies

name: Configuration
description: Weaknesses in this category are typically introduced during the configuration of the product. Security misconfigurations can occur at any level of an application stack, including network services, platform, web server, application server, database, frameworks, and custom code.
mitigations: Implement a repeatable hardening process
Use minimal platform without unnecessary features
Review and update configurations regularly
Use automated configuration scanning
Implement different credentials for different environments

name: Generation of Error Message Containing Sensitive Information
description: The product generates an error message that includes sensitive information about its environment, users, or associated data. Detailed error messages can reveal information useful to an attacker, such as file paths, database structure, stack traces, or configuration details.
mitigations: Implement custom error pages that do not reveal internal details
Log detailed errors server-side only
Return generic error messages to users
Disable debug mode in production
Sanitize error messages before display

name: Insertion of Sensitive Information into Log File
description: Information written to log files can be of a sensitive nature and give valuable guidance to an attacker or expose sensitive user information. Logging passwords, tokens, API keys, personal data, or session IDs can lead to information disclosure.
mitigations: Never log sensitive information (passwords, tokens, PII)
Implement log data classification
Use structured logging with field-level filtering
Protect log files with appropriate access controls
Implement log rotation and secure deletion

name: Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor
description: The product exposes sensitive information to an actor that is not explicitly authorized to have access to that information. This is the general category for information disclosure issues, covering many scenarios from error messages to API responses.
mitigations: Apply the principle of least privilege to information access
Implement proper access controls
Sanitize all output to remove sensitive data
Classify data sensitivity levels
Implement data loss prevention (DLP) measures

name: Exposure of Sensitive System Information to an Unauthorized Control Sphere
description: The product does not properly prevent sensitive system information (such as server version, technology stack, file paths, internal IP addresses) from being accessed by unauthorized actors. This information can be used to plan more targeted attacks.
mitigations: Remove server version headers
Disable directory listing
Use custom error pages
Remove comments and metadata from production code
Sanitize API responses

name: Insertion of Sensitive Information into Externally-Accessible File or Directory
description: The product places sensitive information into files or directories that are accessible to actors who are allowed to have access to the files, but not to the sensitive information. This includes backup files, temporary files, or configuration files left in web-accessible directories.
mitigations: Store sensitive files outside web-accessible directories
Implement proper file permissions
Remove temporary and backup files from production
Use .gitignore and similar to prevent accidental commits
Regularly scan for exposed sensitive files

name: Deserialization of Untrusted Data
description: The product deserializes untrusted data without sufficiently verifying that the resulting data will be valid. Unsafe deserialization can lead to remote code execution, replay attacks, injection attacks, or privilege escalation. This affects languages like Java (ObjectInputStream), Python (pickle), PHP (unserialize), Ruby (Marshal), and .NET (BinaryFormatter).
mitigations: Do not accept serialized objects from untrusted sources
Use data-only serialization formats (JSON, XML) instead of native serialization
Implement integrity checks (HMAC) on serialized data
Restrict deserialization to expected classes (allowlisting)
Use look-ahead deserialization to inspect objects before deserializing
Monitor deserialization activity

name: Improper Restriction of XML External Entity Reference
description: The product processes an XML document that can contain XML entities with URIs that resolve to documents outside of the intended sphere of control, causing the product to embed incorrect documents into its output. XXE attacks can lead to file disclosure, SSRF, denial of service, and in some cases remote code execution.
mitigations: Disable DTD processing entirely
Disable external entity processing
Use less complex data formats (JSON) where possible
Validate and sanitize XML input
Use XML parsers configured to prevent XXE by default

name: Improper Restriction of Recursive Entity References in DTDs ('XML Entity Expansion')
description: The product uses XML documents and allows their structure to be defined with a DTD, but does not properly control the number of recursive entity definitions. This can lead to exponential expansion of entities (billion laughs attack), causing denial of service.
mitigations: Disable DTD processing
Limit entity expansion depth and count
Set XML parser limits for entity expansion
Use alternative data formats

name: Concurrent Execution using Shared Resource with Improper Synchronization ('Race Condition')
description: The product contains a code sequence that can run concurrently with other code, and the code sequence requires temporary, exclusive access to a shared resource, but a timing window exists in which the shared resource can be modified by another code sequence that is operating concurrently.
mitigations: Use proper synchronization primitives (mutexes, semaphores, locks)
Use atomic operations for shared data
Minimize shared state between threads
Use thread-safe data structures
Implement proper locking strategies to prevent deadlocks

name: Time-of-check Time-of-use (TOCTOU) Race Condition
description: The product checks the state of a resource before using that resource, but the resource's state can change between the check and the use in a way that invalidates the results of the check. This can cause the product to perform invalid operations when the resource is in an unexpected state.
mitigations: Use atomic operations that combine check and use
Use file descriptors instead of filenames after initial open
Operate in secure directories that cannot be modified by others
Use proper locking mechanisms

name: Use After Free
description: Referencing memory after it has been freed can cause a program to crash, use unexpected values, or execute code. Use-after-free errors are among the most dangerous memory corruption vulnerabilities and are frequently exploited in real-world attacks.
mitigations: Set pointers to NULL after freeing
Use smart pointers in C++
Use memory-safe languages
Use AddressSanitizer during testing
Implement garbage collection or reference counting

name: NULL Pointer Dereference
description: A NULL pointer dereference occurs when the application dereferences a pointer that it expects to be valid, but is NULL, typically causing a crash or exit. In some cases, NULL pointer dereferences can lead to exploitable conditions.
mitigations: Check pointers for NULL before dereferencing
Use assertions and defensive programming
Use languages with null safety features
Use static analysis to detect potential null dereferences
Use Optional/Maybe types

name: Double Free
description: The product calls free() twice on the same memory address, potentially leading to modification of unexpected memory locations. This can cause the program to crash, corrupt heap metadata, or allow an attacker to execute arbitrary code.
mitigations: Set pointers to NULL after freeing
Use smart pointers in C++
Use memory-safe languages
Use AddressSanitizer during testing
Track allocation and deallocation carefully

name: Improper Restriction of Operations within the Bounds of a Memory Buffer
description: The product performs operations on a memory buffer, but it can read from or write to a memory location that is outside of the intended boundary of the buffer. This is the parent category for many memory corruption vulnerabilities including buffer overflows, out-of-bounds reads/writes.
mitigations: Use memory-safe languages
Use bounds-checking APIs
Enable compiler protections (ASLR, DEP, stack canaries)
Use AddressSanitizer and similar tools during testing

name: Missing Release of Memory after Effective Lifetime
description: The product does not sufficiently track and release allocated memory after it has been used, creating a memory leak. Over time, this can exhaust available memory and lead to denial of service.
mitigations: Use garbage-collected languages when appropriate
Implement proper resource cleanup in error paths
Use RAII pattern in C++
Use memory profiling tools
Implement proper destructor/finalizer patterns

name: Uncontrolled Resource Consumption
description: The product does not properly control the allocation and maintenance of a limited resource, thereby enabling an actor to influence the amount of resources consumed, eventually leading to the exhaustion of available resources. This includes CPU, memory, disk, network connections, and other resources.
mitigations: Implement resource limits and quotas
Use rate limiting
Set timeouts for operations
Monitor resource usage
Implement circuit breakers
Use connection pooling

name: Allocation of Resources Without Limits or Throttling
description: The product allocates a reusable resource or group of resources on behalf of an actor without imposing any restrictions on the size or number of resources that can be allocated, in violation of the intended security policy for that actor.
mitigations: Set hard limits on resource allocation
Implement rate limiting and throttling
Use bounded data structures
Monitor resource allocation patterns
Implement proper cleanup of unused resources

name: Blind Server-Side Request Forgery
description: A variant of SSRF where the attacker cannot directly see the response from the forged request, but can infer information through side channels such as timing, DNS lookups, or out-of-band interactions. The attacker can still use this to scan internal networks, interact with internal services, and exfiltrate data through DNS or other channels.
mitigations: Apply the same mitigations as regular SSRF
Block DNS rebinding
Monitor outbound DNS requests
Use network segmentation
Implement egress filtering

name: Improper Restriction of Rendered UI Layers or Frames
description: The web application does not restrict or incorrectly restricts frame objects or UI layers that belong to another application or domain, which can lead to user confusion about which interface the user is interacting with. This enables clickjacking attacks.
mitigations: Use X-Frame-Options header (DENY or SAMEORIGIN)
Implement Content-Security-Policy frame-ancestors directive
Use frame-busting JavaScript as defense in depth
Require user confirmation for sensitive actions

name: Sensitive Cookie with Improper SameSite Attribute
description: The SameSite attribute for sensitive cookies is not set, or an insecure value is used. The SameSite attribute controls whether cookies are sent with cross-site requests, providing protection against CSRF attacks. Without proper SameSite values, cookies may be sent with requests initiated by third-party websites.
mitigations: Set SameSite=Strict for session cookies when possible
Use SameSite=Lax as a minimum
Combine with anti-CSRF tokens for defense in depth
Set Secure and HttpOnly flags as well

name: Incorrect Permission Assignment for Critical Resource
description: The product specifies permissions for a security-critical resource in a way that allows that resource to be read or modified by unintended actors. This includes overly permissive file permissions, world-readable credentials, and misconfigured access controls on cloud resources.
mitigations: Apply the principle of least privilege to all resources
Use restrictive default permissions
Regularly audit permissions
Use umask to set appropriate default file permissions
Implement infrastructure-as-code with security policies

name: Incorrect Default Permissions
description: During installation or operation, the product sets incorrect permissions for an object that exposes it to an unintended actor. Default permissions that are too permissive can allow unauthorized access to sensitive data or functionality.
mitigations: Set restrictive default permissions
Apply the principle of least privilege
Document and enforce required permissions
Test default configurations for security

name: Initialization with Hard-Coded Network Resource Configuration Data
description: The product initializes data using hard-coded values that act as network resource identifiers. This includes hard-coded URLs, IP addresses, database connection strings, and API endpoints that should be configurable.
mitigations: Use configuration files or environment variables for network resource identifiers
Implement service discovery mechanisms
Use DNS for service resolution
Never hard-code internal network details

name: Exposure of Resource to Wrong Sphere
description: The product exposes a resource to the wrong control sphere, providing unintended actors with inappropriate access to the resource. This is a general weakness covering many scenarios including files, functions, and data exposed to the wrong users or systems.
mitigations: Implement proper access controls
Use principle of least privilege
Segment resources by trust boundary
Validate access at the resource level

name: Cloud Instance Metadata SSRF
description: A specialized form of SSRF targeting cloud instance metadata services (e.g., AWS EC2 metadata at 169.254.169.254, GCP metadata at metadata.google.internal). Successful exploitation can expose IAM credentials, API keys, and other sensitive cloud configuration data.
mitigations: Block requests to metadata IP ranges (169.254.169.254)
Use IMDSv2 (token-based metadata access) on AWS
Implement network-level controls
Use service account restrictions
Apply URL validation against internal IP ranges

name: Improper Neutralization of Argument Delimiters in a Command ('Argument Injection')
description: The product constructs a string for a command to be executed by a separate component in another control sphere, but it does not properly delimit the intended arguments, options, or switches within that command string. Attackers can inject additional arguments to modify command behavior.
mitigations: Use parameterized command execution APIs
Validate and sanitize all command arguments
Use allowlists for permitted argument values
Avoid constructing commands from user input

name: XML Injection (aka Blind XPath Injection)
description: The product does not properly neutralize special elements that are used in XML, allowing attackers to modify the syntax, content, or commands of the XML before it is processed by an end system. This includes modifying XML structure, injecting new elements, and blind XPath injection.
mitigations: Validate and encode XML output
Use parameterized XPath queries
Sanitize user input before XML construction
Use safe XML libraries

name: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements in Output Used by a Downstream Component ('Injection')
description: The product constructs all or part of a command, data structure, or record using externally-influenced input from an upstream component, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes special elements that could modify how it is parsed or interpreted when it is sent to a downstream component. This is the parent category for all injection vulnerabilities.
mitigations: Use parameterized interfaces
Apply output encoding appropriate to the context
Validate input against allowlists
Use type-safe APIs

name: Improper Neutralization of Directives in Dynamically Evaluated Code ('Eval Injection')
description: The product receives input from an upstream component, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes code syntax before using the input in a dynamic evaluation call (e.g., eval). This allows an attacker to execute arbitrary code within the context of the application.
mitigations: Avoid using eval() and similar dynamic code execution functions
Use sandboxed execution environments if dynamic evaluation is necessary
Validate input strictly against allowlists
Use AST-based parsing instead of eval

name: Improper Neutralization of CRLF Sequences in HTTP Headers ('HTTP Response Splitting')
description: The product receives data from an HTTP agent/component (e.g., web browser, web server, proxy), but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes CR and LF characters before the data is included in outgoing HTTP headers. Attackers can inject additional HTTP headers or split the response to perform XSS, cache poisoning, or redirect attacks.
mitigations: Validate and sanitize all data used in HTTP headers
Strip or encode CR and LF characters from header values
Use framework-provided header setting methods
Implement web application firewall rules

name: Improper Neutralization of Formula Elements in a CSV File
description: The product saves user-provided information into a Comma-Separated Value (CSV) file, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes special elements that could be interpreted as a command when the file is opened by a spreadsheet program. CSV injection can lead to code execution when the file is opened in Excel or similar applications.
mitigations: Prefix cells starting with =, +, -, @, tab, or carriage return with a single quote
Validate and sanitize data before CSV export
Warn users about the risks of opening CSV files from untrusted sources
Use tab-delimited format as an alternative

name: Execution with Unnecessary Privileges
description: The product performs an operation at a privilege level that is higher than the minimum level required, which creates new weaknesses or amplifies the consequences of other weaknesses. Running services as root/admin when not needed increases the impact of any vulnerability.
mitigations: Apply the principle of least privilege
Drop privileges as soon as possible
Use separate service accounts with minimal permissions
Implement privilege separation
Use capabilities instead of full root access

name: Improperly Controlled Modification of Dynamically-Determined Object Attributes
description: The product receives input from an upstream component that specifies multiple attributes, properties, or fields that are to be initialized or updated in an object, but it does not properly control which attributes can be modified. This is commonly known as mass assignment or autobinding vulnerability, prevalent in web frameworks like Rails, Django, and Express.js.
mitigations: Use allowlists to specify which attributes can be modified
Never directly bind request parameters to model objects
Use separate DTOs/view models for input
Implement strong parameter filtering

name: OWASP Top Ten 2017 Category A9 - Using Components with Known Vulnerabilities
description: The product uses third-party components (libraries, frameworks, or other software modules) that have known security vulnerabilities. Attackers can exploit these known vulnerabilities to compromise the application. This is one of the most prevalent and impactful weaknesses in modern software.
mitigations: Maintain an inventory of all third-party components and versions
Regularly scan for known vulnerabilities using SCA tools
Subscribe to security advisories for used components
Implement automated dependency updates
Remove unused dependencies
Use lock files to pin dependency versions

name: Inclusion of Functionality from Untrusted Control Sphere
description: The product imports, requires, or includes executable functionality (such as a library or script) from a source that is outside of the intended control sphere. This includes loading code from untrusted CDNs, package registries, or third-party sources without integrity verification.
mitigations: Verify integrity of included code using checksums or signatures
Use Subresource Integrity (SRI) for CDN resources
Pin dependency versions
Use private package registries
Audit third-party code before inclusion
Implement Content Security Policy

name: Download of Code Without Integrity Check
description: The product downloads source code or an executable from a remote location and executes the code without sufficiently verifying the origin and integrity of the code. This is a critical supply chain security issue.
mitigations: Verify code integrity using cryptographic signatures or checksums
Use HTTPS for all code downloads
Implement certificate pinning
Use package managers with signature verification
Audit downloaded code before execution

name: Permissive Cross-domain Policy with Untrusted Domains
description: The product uses a cross-domain policy file (such as CORS configuration, crossdomain.xml, or clientaccesspolicy.xml) that grants access to a wider range of domains than intended. Overly permissive CORS policies can allow unauthorized cross-origin data access.
mitigations: Restrict CORS origins to specific trusted domains
Never use Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * for authenticated endpoints
Validate Origin header against an allowlist
Avoid reflecting the Origin header in Access-Control-Allow-Origin
Be restrictive with Access-Control-Allow-Credentials

name: Use of Unmaintained Third Party Components
description: The product relies on third-party components that are not actively maintained or have been abandoned by their developers. Unmaintained components may contain unpatched vulnerabilities and will not receive security updates.
mitigations: Regularly audit dependencies for maintenance status
Replace unmaintained components with maintained alternatives
Fork and maintain critical unmaintained dependencies
Use automated tools to monitor dependency health

name: Weak Password Requirements
description: The product does not require that users should have strong passwords, which makes it easier for attackers to compromise user accounts. This includes allowing short passwords, common passwords, or passwords without complexity requirements.
mitigations: Enforce minimum password length (at least 8-12 characters)
Check passwords against known breached password lists
Implement password complexity requirements
Use password strength meters
Implement multi-factor authentication as additional protection

name: Weak Password Recovery Mechanism for Forgotten Password
description: The product contains a mechanism for users to recover or change their passwords without knowing the original password, but the mechanism is weak. This includes easily guessable security questions, password reset tokens sent via insecure channels, or predictable reset URLs.
mitigations: Use time-limited, single-use password reset tokens
Send reset links via verified email addresses
Implement rate limiting on reset requests
Avoid security questions
Require additional verification for password reset
Invalidate all sessions after password change

name: Authentication Bypass Using an Alternate Path or Channel
description: A product requires authentication, but the product has an alternate path or channel that does not require authentication. Attackers can bypass authentication by finding unprotected API endpoints, debug interfaces, or alternative access paths.
mitigations: Ensure all access paths require authentication
Use centralized authentication enforcement
Conduct thorough security testing of all endpoints
Remove debug and test endpoints from production
Implement defense in depth

name: JWT Security Issues
description: Security weaknesses related to JSON Web Token (JWT) implementation, including missing signature verification, algorithm confusion attacks (alg:none), weak signing keys, and failure to validate claims (expiration, issuer, audience). JWT misuse can lead to authentication bypass and privilege escalation.
mitigations: Always verify JWT signatures
Reject tokens with 'alg: none'
Use strong signing keys (at least 256 bits for HMAC)
Validate all claims (exp, iss, aud, nbf)
Use asymmetric algorithms (RS256, ES256) for distributed systems
Implement token revocation mechanisms
Set short expiration times

name: Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity
description: The product does not sufficiently verify the origin or authenticity of data, in a way that causes it to accept invalid data. This can lead to trusting data from untrusted sources, accepting forged requests, or processing tampered data.
mitigations: Implement integrity checks (HMAC, digital signatures)
Verify data origin using cryptographic methods
Use mutual TLS for service-to-service communication
Validate all input from external sources

name: Origin Validation Error
description: The product does not properly verify that the source of data or communication is valid. This allows attackers to access functionality or data by spoofing the origin of requests, bypassing cross-origin policies, or abusing trust relationships.
mitigations: Validate Origin and Referer headers
Implement CORS properly
Use anti-CSRF tokens
Verify request sources cryptographically

name: Files or Directories Accessible to External Parties
description: The product makes files or directories accessible to unauthorized actors, even though they should not be. This includes exposing .git directories, backup files, configuration files, or source code through web servers.
mitigations: Configure web servers to deny access to sensitive directories
Remove unnecessary files from web-accessible directories
Use .htaccess or server configuration to block access to sensitive file types
Implement proper access controls
Regularly scan for exposed files

name: Uncontrolled Search Path Element
description: The product uses a fixed or controlled search path to find resources, but one or more locations in that path can be under the control of unintended actors. This can lead to DLL hijacking, library injection, or path manipulation attacks.
mitigations: Use absolute paths for loading libraries and resources
Validate search paths
Remove current directory from search paths
Use secure library loading functions
Implement code signing for loaded modules

name: Indirect OS Command Injection via File or Environment
description: The product reads data from a file, environment variable, or other indirect source and uses this data to construct and execute an OS command. Even though the product does not directly accept user input for the command, if the attacker can control the indirect source, they can inject commands.
mitigations: Validate data from all sources, not just direct user input
Use parameterized command execution
Treat environment variables and file contents as untrusted input
Use allowlists for permitted command arguments

name: DNS Rebinding Attack
description: An attack technique that can be used to bypass SSRF protections by exploiting DNS resolution behavior. The attacker controls a DNS record that initially resolves to an allowed IP address but changes to an internal IP address between the time of validation and the time of use.
mitigations: Validate resolved IP addresses, not just hostnames
Re-validate IP addresses after DNS resolution
Pin DNS resolution results
Block private IP ranges at the network level
Use short DNS TTLs for validation

name: Horizontal Privilege Escalation
description: A user can access resources or perform actions on behalf of another user at the same privilege level. This typically occurs when the application uses user-controllable identifiers (such as IDs in URLs or form fields) to access resources without verifying that the authenticated user owns the resource.
common_consequences: Unauthorized data access across user accounts
Data manipulation of other users' data
mitigations: Verify resource ownership on every access
Use the authenticated user's identity from the session, not from request parameters
Implement object-level authorization
Use indirect reference maps

name: Vertical Privilege Escalation
description: A lower-privileged user can access functions or data reserved for higher-privileged users, such as administrative functions. This can occur due to missing role checks, client-side-only authorization enforcement, or insecure direct object references to administrative resources.
mitigations: Enforce authorization checks on the server side
Implement role-based access control with proper hierarchy
Never rely on client-side authorization checks
Test all administrative functions for access control
Implement centralized authorization enforcement

name: Expected Behavior Violation
description: A feature, API, or function does not perform according to its specification or documentation, which can lead to incorrect usage and security vulnerabilities. This includes undocumented behavior, inconsistent error handling, and silent failures.
mitigations: Ensure implementation matches specification
Document all behavior including edge cases
Implement comprehensive testing
Use design by contract principles

name: Improper Enforcement of Behavioral Workflow
description: The product supports a session in which more than one behavior must be performed by an actor, but it does not properly ensure that the actor performs the behaviors in the required sequence. This can allow attackers to skip steps in a workflow, such as bypassing payment or approval processes.
mitigations: Enforce workflow state on the server side
Validate that all required steps have been completed
Use state machines to model workflows
Implement server-side session tracking of workflow state

name: Improper Control of Interaction Frequency
description: The product does not properly limit the number or frequency of interactions that it has with an actor, such as the number of incoming requests. This allows attackers to perform denial-of-service attacks, brute-force attacks, or resource exhaustion through excessive requests.
mitigations: Implement rate limiting
Use throttling mechanisms
Implement CAPTCHA for sensitive operations
Use exponential backoff
Implement IP-based and account-based rate limits

name: Business Logic Errors
description: Weaknesses in business logic can be exploited to circumvent security controls, manipulate pricing, bypass restrictions, or abuse application functionality in ways not intended by the developers. These are often specific to the application's domain and cannot be detected by automated tools alone.
mitigations: Implement thorough business rule validation
Use server-side enforcement of all business rules
Conduct threat modeling specific to business processes
Perform business logic testing
Implement monitoring for anomalous business transactions

name: Improper Authorization
description: The product does not perform or incorrectly performs an authorization check when an actor attempts to access a resource or perform an action. This is a broad category covering both missing and incorrect authorization implementations.
mitigations: Implement consistent authorization checks
Use a centralized authorization framework
Apply deny-by-default access policies
Test authorization thoroughly

name: Improper Check for Unusual or Exceptional Conditions
description: The product does not check or improperly checks for unusual or exceptional conditions that are not expected to occur frequently during day-to-day operation. This can lead to unexpected behavior, crashes, or security vulnerabilities when those conditions arise.
mitigations: Handle all error conditions explicitly
Use defensive programming techniques
Implement proper exception handling
Test error paths and boundary conditions
Use static analysis to find unchecked conditions

name: Improper Handling of Exceptional Conditions
description: The product does not handle or incorrectly handles an exceptional condition. When exceptions occur but are not properly handled, the product may enter an inconsistent state, crash, or leak sensitive information through error messages.
mitigations: Implement comprehensive exception handling
Use try-catch-finally blocks appropriately
Never ignore caught exceptions
Log exceptions securely without exposing sensitive data
Implement global exception handlers

name: Unquoted Search Path or Element
description: The product uses a search path that contains an unquoted element, in which the element can be resolved to more than one target. On Windows, unquoted service paths can be exploited for privilege escalation when a path contains spaces.
mitigations: Quote all file paths that contain spaces
Use absolute paths
Validate path elements before use
Review service configurations for unquoted paths

name: Improper Link Resolution Before File Access ('Link Following')
description: The product attempts to access a file based on the filename, but it does not properly prevent that filename from identifying a link or shortcut that resolves to an unintended resource. Symlink attacks can allow attackers to read, write, or delete files outside the intended scope.
mitigations: Use canonicalized paths
Check file type before access (use lstat not stat)
Operate in controlled directories
Use O_NOFOLLOW flag when opening files
Drop privileges before file operations

name: Insecure Temporary File
description: Creating and using insecure temporary files can leave application and system data vulnerable to attack. Temporary files created with predictable names, in shared directories, or without proper permissions can be exploited by attackers to read sensitive data or inject malicious content.
mitigations: Use secure temporary file creation functions (mkstemp, tmpfile)
Create temporary files in restricted directories
Set restrictive permissions on temporary files
Clean up temporary files promptly
Use unpredictable file names

name: Insecure Storage of Sensitive Information
description: The product stores sensitive information without properly limiting read or write access by unauthorized actors. This includes storing sensitive data in world-readable files, shared storage, or client-side storage without encryption.
mitigations: Encrypt sensitive data at rest
Apply proper access controls to storage
Use platform-provided secure storage (Keychain, Credential Store)
Avoid storing sensitive data in client-side storage
Implement data classification

name: Exposure of Private Personal Information to an Unauthorized Actor
description: The product does not properly prevent a person's private, personal information from being accessed by actors who either are not explicitly authorized to access the information or do not have the implicit consent of the person about whom the information is collected. This is particularly relevant for GDPR, CCPA, and other privacy regulations.
mitigations: Implement data minimization principles
Apply access controls to personal data
Encrypt personal data at rest and in transit
Implement data anonymization or pseudonymization
Conduct privacy impact assessments
Implement consent management

name: Observable Timing Discrepancy
description: Two separate operations in a product require different amounts of time to complete, in a way that is observable to an actor and reveals security-relevant information about the state of the product, such as whether a particular operation was successful. Timing side channels can be used to enumerate valid usernames, bypass authentication, or extract cryptographic keys.
mitigations: Use constant-time comparison functions for security-sensitive comparisons
Return consistent response times regardless of success/failure
Use constant-time cryptographic operations
Add artificial delays to normalize response times

name: Misconfigured CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing)
description: The application implements CORS headers in a way that is overly permissive, such as reflecting any origin, using wildcard origins with credentials, or allowing all methods and headers. This can allow malicious websites to make authenticated cross-origin requests.
mitigations: Use an allowlist of trusted origins
Never reflect arbitrary origins with credentials
Avoid using wildcard (*) with Access-Control-Allow-Credentials
Restrict allowed methods and headers
Implement proper preflight request handling

name: Improper Output Neutralization for Logs
description: The product does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes output that is written to logs. This allows attackers to forge log entries or inject malicious content into logs (log injection), potentially misleading forensic analysis or exploiting log processing systems.
mitigations: Sanitize all data before writing to logs
Encode newline characters and other control characters
Use structured logging formats
Validate log input against expected formats
Implement log integrity checking

name: Improper Verification of Source of a Communication Channel
description: The product establishes a communication channel to handle an incoming request that has been initiated by an actor, but it does not properly verify that the request is coming from the expected origin. This can allow man-in-the-middle attacks or impersonation.
mitigations: Implement mutual TLS authentication
Verify origin of incoming connections
Use certificate pinning
Implement proper handshake protocols

name: Improper Authorization in Handler for Custom URL Scheme
description: The product uses a handler for a custom URL scheme, but it does not properly restrict which actors can invoke the handler using the custom URL scheme. On mobile platforms, this can allow malicious apps to invoke privileged actions.
mitigations: Validate the source of URL scheme invocations
Use universal links / app links instead of custom URL schemes
Require user confirmation for sensitive actions triggered via URL schemes
Implement proper authorization checks in URL scheme handlers

name: Exposed API Keys and Secrets
description: API keys, tokens, passwords, or other secrets are exposed in source code, configuration files committed to version control, client-side code, or public repositories. This is one of the most common causes of data breaches in modern applications.
mitigations: Use environment variables or secrets management services (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault)
Never commit secrets to version control
Use .gitignore to exclude sensitive files
Implement pre-commit hooks to detect secrets
Rotate exposed credentials immediately
Use separate credentials for each environment
detection_methods: Secret scanning (truffleHog, git-secrets, GitHub secret scanning)

name: Inefficient Regular Expression Complexity (ReDoS)
description: The product uses a regular expression with an inefficient, possibly exponential worst-case computational complexity that consumes excessive CPU cycles. An attacker can craft input that triggers catastrophic backtracking, causing the application to hang or become unresponsive (Regular Expression Denial of Service).
mitigations: Avoid nested quantifiers and overlapping alternations
Use atomic groups or possessive quantifiers
Set timeouts on regex execution
Use RE2 or other regex engines that guarantee linear-time matching
Test regular expressions with adversarial inputs
Limit input length before regex processing

name: Weak Authentication
description: The product uses an authentication mechanism to restrict access to specific users or identities, but the mechanism does not sufficiently prove that an actor has successfully authenticated. This includes relying on easily spoofable values (IP addresses, user agents) or client-side-only authentication.
mitigations: Use strong, proven authentication mechanisms
Never rely on client-side authentication alone
Implement multi-factor authentication
Use cryptographic authentication methods

name: Improper Resource Shutdown or Release
description: The product does not release or incorrectly releases a resource before it is made available for re-use. This can lead to resource exhaustion, including file descriptor leaks, connection leaks, and memory leaks.
mitigations: Use try-with-resources or equivalent constructs
Implement proper cleanup in finally blocks
Use RAII pattern in C++
Implement connection pooling with proper cleanup
Monitor resource usage

name: Broken Object Level Authorization (BOLA)
description: APIs do not properly validate that the authenticated user has appropriate authorization to access the specific object being requested. This is the most common API security vulnerability, where an attacker can manipulate object IDs in API requests to access data belonging to other users. Also known as IDOR in the API context.
mitigations: Implement object-level authorization checks for every API endpoint
Use the authenticated user's identity from the session/token
Do not rely on client-supplied object IDs without authorization checks
Use random and unpredictable IDs (UUIDs)
Write comprehensive authorization tests

name: Broken Function Level Authorization
description: The application does not properly enforce authorization checks at the function level, allowing regular users to access administrative functions. This commonly occurs when API endpoints for admin functions are exposed without role-based access checks.
common_consequences: Administrative function access by regular users
mitigations: Implement role-based access control for all functions
Deny access by default
Use centralized authorization middleware
Test all endpoints with different role levels
Separate admin and user API routes

name: PDF-Based SSRF and Data Exfiltration
description: Server-side PDF generation using user-controlled input can lead to SSRF when the PDF generator processes URLs, iframes, or other network-fetching constructs embedded in the HTML/CSS input. Attackers can use this to access internal services or exfiltrate data.
mitigations: Sanitize HTML input before PDF generation
Disable network access in PDF generators
Use sandboxed PDF generation
Block access to internal IP ranges
Use allowlists for external resources

name: External Control of Assumed-Immutable Web Parameter
description: The web application does not sufficiently verify inputs that are assumed to be immutable but are actually externally controllable, such as hidden form fields, cookies, URL parameters, or HTTP headers. Attackers can modify these values to bypass security controls.
mitigations: Never trust client-side data for security decisions
Validate all inputs on the server side
Use server-side session storage for security-sensitive data
Sign and verify parameters that must not be modified

name: Use of Persistent Cookies Containing Sensitive Information
description: The web application uses persistent cookies to store sensitive information. Persistent cookies remain on the user's system even after the browser is closed, increasing the window for cookie theft and unauthorized access.
mitigations: Use session cookies (no Expires/Max-Age) for sensitive data
Minimize data stored in cookies
Encrypt cookie values
Set appropriate expiration times
Use server-side session storage

name: Plaintext Storage of a Password
description: Storing a password in plaintext may result in a system compromise. If attackers gain access to the password store (database, file, etc.), they immediately have access to all user credentials without any additional effort.
mitigations: Use adaptive hashing algorithms (bcrypt, scrypt, Argon2id)
Use unique salts per password
Never store passwords in plaintext or with reversible encryption
Implement password hashing in a dedicated authentication service

name: Predictable Session Identifiers
description: The product generates session identifiers using a predictable algorithm or insufficient randomness, making it possible for attackers to guess valid session IDs and hijack user sessions.
mitigations: Use cryptographically secure random number generators for session IDs
Ensure session IDs have sufficient length and entropy (at least 128 bits)
Use framework-provided session management
Implement session ID rotation

name: Binding to an Unrestricted IP Address
description: The product binds a network service to an unrestricted IP address (0.0.0.0 or ::), making the service accessible on all network interfaces including external ones, when it should only be accessible on specific interfaces (e.g., localhost).
mitigations: Bind services to specific IP addresses
Use localhost (127.0.0.1) for local-only services
Implement firewall rules to restrict access
Use network segmentation

name: Insufficient Logging
description: When a security-critical event occurs, the product either does not record the event or omits important details about the event when logging it. Insufficient logging hinders forensic analysis and incident response.
mitigations: Log all authentication attempts (success and failure)
Log authorization failures
Log input validation failures
Include relevant context in log entries (timestamp, user, IP, action)
Implement centralized log management
Set up alerting for security events

name: GraphQL Injection and Abuse
description: GraphQL APIs can be vulnerable to various attacks including injection through query variables, introspection abuse revealing schema details, deeply nested queries causing denial of service, and batch query attacks bypassing rate limits.
common_consequences: Denial of service via complex queries
mitigations: Disable introspection in production
Implement query depth and complexity limits
Use query allowlisting (persisted queries)
Implement proper input validation for variables
Apply rate limiting per query
Use cost analysis for queries

name: Missing Webhook Signature Verification
description: The application receives webhook callbacks from external services but does not verify the signature or authenticity of the incoming webhook payload. This allows attackers to send forged webhook events to trigger unintended actions.
mitigations: Verify webhook signatures using HMAC or similar
Validate webhook source IP addresses
Use shared secrets for webhook verification
Implement idempotency for webhook processing
Verify timestamps to prevent replay attacks

name: Unsafe YAML Deserialization
description: The application uses YAML deserialization with unsafe loaders (e.g., yaml.load() in Python without SafeLoader, or YAML.load in Ruby without safe_load) that can instantiate arbitrary objects and execute code through YAML tags like !!python/object.
mitigations: Use safe YAML loaders (yaml.safe_load in Python, YAML.safe_load in Ruby)
Never use yaml.load() or yaml.unsafe_load() with untrusted input
Validate YAML structure after loading
Use JSON instead of YAML for untrusted input

name: Unsafe Python Pickle Deserialization
description: The application deserializes data using Python's pickle module from untrusted sources. Pickle can execute arbitrary Python code during deserialization through the __reduce__ method, making it inherently unsafe for untrusted data.
mitigations: Never unpickle data from untrusted sources
Use JSON or MessagePack for data interchange
If pickle is required, implement __reduce__ restrictions
Use hmac to verify pickle integrity from trusted sources
Consider using restricted unpickler

name: XXE-Based Server-Side Request Forgery
description: XML External Entity (XXE) processing can be used to perform SSRF attacks by defining external entities that reference internal URLs, cloud metadata endpoints, or internal services. This combines the risks of both XXE and SSRF.
mitigations: Disable DTD and external entity processing
Use JSON instead of XML
Implement network egress controls
Block access to metadata endpoints from XML processors

name: File System Race Condition
description: A race condition exists in file system operations where the time between checking file properties (existence, permissions, ownership) and using the file creates a window during which an attacker can modify the file or create a symlink, leading to unauthorized access.
mitigations: Use atomic file operations
Use file descriptors instead of pathnames after initial open
Set proper directory permissions to prevent race conditions
Use O_NOFOLLOW and related flags

name: Improper Synchronization
description: The product utilizes multiple threads or processes to allow temporary access to a shared resource that can only be exclusive to one process at a time, but it does not properly synchronize these accesses, which can lead to simultaneous accesses of this resource by multiple threads or processes.
mitigations: Use proper synchronization primitives
Minimize shared state
Use thread-safe data structures
Implement proper lock ordering to prevent deadlocks

name: Prototype Pollution
description: In JavaScript environments, an attacker can inject properties into Object.prototype or other built-in prototypes through vulnerable merge/extend/clone operations. This can lead to property injection across all objects, potentially causing authentication bypass, remote code execution, or denial of service.
mitigations: Use Object.create(null) for dictionary objects
Freeze Object.prototype
Validate keys when merging objects (reject __proto__, constructor, prototype)
Use Map instead of plain objects for user-controlled keys
Use libraries that protect against prototype pollution

name: Improperly Controlled Modification of Object Prototype Attributes ('Prototype Pollution')
description: The product receives input from an upstream component that specifies attributes that are to be initialized or updated in an object, but it does not properly control modifications to the prototype of the object, leading to prototype pollution in JavaScript/Node.js applications.
mitigations: Validate and sanitize property keys
Use Object.create(null) for dictionaries
Freeze prototypes in critical applications
Use safe deep-merge libraries
Validate JSON schema before processing

name: Access of Resource Using Incompatible Type ('Type Confusion')
description: The product allocates or initializes a resource such as a pointer, object, or variable using one type, but it later accesses that resource using a type that is incompatible with the original type. In C/C++, this can lead to memory corruption and code execution.
mitigations: Use type-safe languages
Implement strict type checking
Use safe casting functions
Enable compiler warnings for type issues

name: Untrusted Search Path
description: The product searches for critical resources using an externally-supplied search path that can point to resources that are not under the product's direct control. This can be exploited for DLL hijacking, PATH manipulation, or loading malicious modules.
mitigations: Use absolute paths for critical resources
Validate all search path entries
Remove untrusted directories from search paths
Use secure library loading mechanisms

name: Authentication Bypass by Spoofing
description: The product performs authentication based on the name or identifier of a resource (such as an IP address, hostname, or a user agent string) rather than on secure, unforgeable credentials. Spoofing these identifiers can bypass authentication.
mitigations: Do not use spoofable identifiers for authentication
Use cryptographic authentication
Implement proper credential-based authentication
Use additional verification methods

name: Modification of Assumed-Immutable Data (MAID)
description: The product does not properly protect modification of data that is assumed to be immutable. In web applications, this includes modifying hidden form fields, cookies, HTTP headers, or other parameters that the application assumes cannot be changed by the user.
mitigations: Never trust client-supplied data
Implement server-side validation
Use cryptographic integrity checks for data that must not be modified
Store immutable data server-side
